title: Leather paring, thinning book cover leather for tight turns
subtitle: How to reduce leather thickness at the edges and hinges for clean turn-ins
excerpt: Full-thickness book leather is 1.2-2 mm thick. At turn-ins and hinges this causes bunching at the corners. Paring, skiving the leather thinner, solves this. The technique needs a sharp paring knife, a solid surface, and patience.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on leather preparation: detailed description of paring technique on a lithographic stone with the French paring knife, with guidance on grain direction, moisture, and the angle of the blade.

glossaryTerms.definition: The act of thinning leather at its edges by shaving it at a low angle with a sharp knife. Also called skiving.
The outer surface of the leather, the side that was the skin's exterior. Smooth, tight, and tougher than the flesh side.
The inner surface of the leather, the side that was against the animal's flesh. Suede-like in texture; the side pared when skiving.

is required wherever leather must bend sharply: at corners (which are the most demanding), at the turn-in edges, and optionally at the joint hinge.

attrs.title: Cutting tool safety
attrs.body: Leather paring is one of the highest-risk cuts in bookbinding. The knife is driven at a low angle directly across the material toward the hand holding the leather. Never let the knife travel toward any part of the body. Wear a cut-resistant glove on the holding hand. Change the blade or resharpen before starting, a dull blade requires more pressure and is more likely to slip.

items.name: Book leather
items.notes: Vegetable-tanned calf, goat, or sheep, 1.0-2.0 mm finished thickness. Chrome-tanned leather is not used in traditional hand bookbinding.

items.name: Craft knife with a fresh blade
items.notes: A Swann-Morton 10A scalpel blade or a dedicated paring knife (the French paring knife with its wide, thin blade) is ideal. A standard craft knife works at the cost of more effort.

items.name: Cutting mat, A3
items.notes: Or better: a sheet of zinc or a litho stone (the traditional paring surface). The mat is softer than ideal but works at home scale.

items.name: Glass or smooth board
items.notes: For dampening the leather slightly before paring.

content.text: Lightly dampen the area to be pared with a sponge dipped in clean water. The leather should be slightly damp, not wet, water-logged leather tears rather than shaves. Allow thirty seconds for the moisture to penetrate before starting. Dry leather is harder and the blade tends to jump rather than slice cleanly.

Hold the leather, grain-side down, firmly on the cutting mat. Place the blade at a very low angle, almost flat to the surface, around 10-15° from horizontal. Drive the blade steadily forward in a long, smooth stroke, shaving from the edge inward. The first pass removes a thin layer; aim for four or five passes to reduce the edge to roughly 0.3-0.4 mm. At corners, pare at 45° to the corner point to thin the very tip.

Hold the pared edge up to a light source. It should be visibly translucent and uniform in thickness. A ridge or thick spot will show as an opaque patch. Run a finger across the flesh side, the transition from full thickness to pared should be gradual, not a sharp step. A step creates a visible ridge on the grain side after the turn-in.

items.symptom: The blade cuts through the leather rather than shaving it
items.cause: Blade angle is too steep, or the leather is too wet.
items.fix: Lower the blade angle further and let the leather dry for another minute before continuing.

items.symptom: The shaved area is uneven, thick in the centre, thin at the edges
items.cause: The blade was not held at a consistent low angle throughout the stroke.
items.fix: Use a paring guide, a strip of card taped at the correct angle, to train the hand. Consistency comes with repetition.
